message:
test(manifest): Add test cases for C++ and Ruby security fixtures

This commit adds manifest entries for the new C++ macro/I/O fixtures and
Ruby security/serialization fixtures, enabling automated regression testing.

Context: Expanding Test Manifest Coverage
==========================================
The test manifest drives automated verification of UBS detection capabilities.
This update adds 6 new test cases covering C++ and Ruby patterns.

New Test Cases Overview
========================

**C++ fixtures** (2 cases):
1. cpp-macro-traps (buggy)
2. cpp-modern-io-clean (clean baseline)

**Ruby fixtures** (4 cases):
3. ruby-taint-buggy (command injection)
4. ruby-taint-clean (secure command execution)
5. ruby-serialization-buggy (YAML.load)
6. ruby-serialization-clean (YAML.safe_load)

Test Case 1: cpp-macro-traps
=============================

**Purpose**: Verify C++ macro detection rules work correctly.

**Manifest entry**:
```json
{
  "id": "cpp-macro-traps",
  "description": "C++ macro pitfalls (min/max macros, debug toggles).",
  "path": "test-suite/cpp/macro_traps",
  "language": "cpp",
  "tags": ["cpp", "macros", "buggy"],
  "args": ["--only=cpp", "--fail-on-warning"],
  "expect": {
    "exit_code": "nonzero",
    "totals": {
      "warning": {"min": 1}
    },
    "require_substrings": [
      "min/max macros detected"
    ]
  }
}
```

**Expectations**:
- **Exit code**: nonzero (because warnings exist)
- **Warnings**: At least 1 (for min/max macro detection)
- **Output contains**: "min/max macros detected"

**Why these assertions**:
- Verifies macro detection rules trigger
- Ensures warning severity is applied
- Validates output message format

**Failure scenario**:
If macro detection is broken, test fails with:
```
AssertionError: Expected at least 1 warning, got 0
```

Test Case 2: cpp-modern-io-clean
=================================

**Purpose**: Verify no false positives on modern C++ code.

**Manifest entry**:
```json
{
  "id": "cpp-modern-io-clean",
  "description": "C++ fmt/iostream clean baseline with no legacy I/O APIs.",
  "path": "test-suite/cpp/modern_io",
  "language": "cpp",
  "tags": ["cpp", "clean", "modern"],
  "args": ["--only=cpp"],
  "expect": {
    "exit_code": "zero",
    "totals": {
      "warning": {"max": 0}
    }
  }
}
```

**Expectations**:
- **Exit code**: zero (no warnings)
- **Warnings**: Exactly 0 (clean baseline)

**Why this matters**:
- Prevents false positives on std::format, std::cout
- Verifies modern C++ patterns are recognized as safe
- Baseline for "this is how it should be done"

**Failure scenario**:
If false positive occurs:
```
AssertionError: Expected 0 warnings, got 1
Warning: iostream usage detected (false positive!)
```

Test Case 3: ruby-taint-buggy
==============================

**Purpose**: Verify command injection detection in Ruby.

**Manifest entry**:
```json
{
  "id": "ruby-taint-buggy",
  "description": "Ruby taint regression (single-string system/backticks).",
  "path": "test-suite/ruby/security_buggy",
  "language": "ruby",
  "tags": ["ruby", "security", "buggy"],
  "args": ["--only=ruby", "--fail-on-warning"],
  "expect": {
    "exit_code": "nonzero",
    "totals": {
      "critical": {"min": 1}
    },
    "require_substrings": [
      "Shell invocation risk",
      "Backtick command execution"
    ]
  }
}
```

**Expectations**:
- **Exit code**: nonzero (critical issues exist)
- **Critical findings**: At least 1 (security vulnerability)
- **Output contains**: Both "Shell invocation risk" and "Backtick command execution"

**Why two substrings**:
- Verifies both system() and backtick detection
- Ensures all vulnerabilities in fixture are caught
- Prevents regression if one pattern breaks

**Failure scenario**:
If only one vulnerability detected:
```
AssertionError: Required substring 'Backtick command execution' not found
```

Test Case 4: ruby-taint-clean
==============================

**Purpose**: Verify secure Ruby patterns don't trigger false positives.

**Manifest entry**:
```json
{
  "id": "ruby-taint-clean",
  "description": "Ruby secure shell calls using argv arrays/Open3.",
  "path": "test-suite/ruby/security_clean",
  "language": "ruby",
  "tags": ["ruby", "security", "clean"],
  "args": ["--only=ruby"],
  "expect": {
    "exit_code": "zero",
    "totals": {
      "critical": {"max": 0},
      "warning": {"max": 0}
    }
  }
}
```

**Expectations**:
- **Exit code**: zero (no findings)
- **Critical findings**: 0 (secure pattern)
- **Warnings**: 0 (no issues at all)

**Why strict (max: 0)**:
- Argv arrays are definitively safe
- Open3.capture2 is the recommended secure API
- Any warning here is a false positive

**Failure scenario**:
If false positive occurs:
```
AssertionError: Expected 0 warnings, got 1
Warning: system() detected (but it's the safe argv form!)
```

Test Case 5: ruby-serialization-buggy
======================================

**Purpose**: Verify YAML deserialization vulnerability detection.

**Manifest entry**:
```json
{
  "id": "ruby-serialization-buggy",
  "description": "Ruby unsafe YAML/Marshal loading regression.",
  "path": "test-suite/ruby/serialization_buggy",
  "language": "ruby",
  "tags": ["ruby", "serialization", "buggy"],
  "args": ["--only=ruby", "--fail-on-warning"],
  "expect": {
    "exit_code": "nonzero",
    "totals": {
      "critical": {"min": 1}
    },
    "require_substrings": [
      "YAML",
      "deserialization"
    ]
  }
}
```

**Expectations**:
- **Exit code**: nonzero (critical vulnerability)
- **Critical findings**: At least 1 (RCE vulnerability)
- **Output contains**: "YAML" and "deserialization"

**Why these substrings**:
- Flexible matching (allows various message formats)
- Core concepts that must appear in warning
- Avoids being too specific (brittle test)

**Failure scenario**:
If YAML.load not detected:
```
AssertionError: Expected at least 1 critical finding, got 0
```

Test Case 6: ruby-serialization-clean
======================================

**Purpose**: Verify YAML.safe_load doesn't trigger false positives.

**Manifest entry**:
```json
{
  "id": "ruby-serialization-clean",
  "description": "Ruby safe YAML loading baseline.",
  "path": "test-suite/ruby/serialization_clean",
  "language": "ruby",
  "tags": ["ruby", "serialization", "clean"],
  "args": ["--only=ruby"],
  "expect": {
    "exit_code": "zero",
    "totals": {
      "critical": {"max": 0},
      "warning": {"max": 0}
    }
  }
}
```

**Expectations**:
- **Exit code**: zero (no issues)
- **Critical findings**: 0 (safe API)
- **Warnings**: 0 (completely clean)

**Why strict (max: 0)**:
- YAML.safe_load with permitted_classes is the secure pattern
- Any warning here indicates detection logic is too broad
- Helps tune rules to avoid false positives

Manifest Structure Updates
===========================

**Insertion points**:
- C++ cases inserted after existing C++ tests (line ~933)
- Ruby cases inserted after existing Ruby tests (line ~1460)

**Why this placement**:
- Groups tests by language (easier to navigate)
- Maintains existing ordering convention
- New tests at end of language sections

**Tag usage**:
- `buggy`: Expected to trigger warnings
- `clean`: Expected to produce zero warnings
- `security`: Security-related patterns
- `serialization`: Deserialization patterns
- `macros`: C++ macro-specific

**Tags enable**:
```bash
# Run only security tests
./test-suite/run_manifest.py --tag security

# Run only clean baselines
./test-suite/run_manifest.py --tag clean
```

Test Execution
==============

**Running new tests**:
```bash
# Run specific test
uv run python test-suite/run_manifest.py --case cpp-macro-traps

# Run all C++ tests
uv run python test-suite/run_manifest.py --language cpp

# Run all Ruby security tests
uv run python test-suite/run_manifest.py --tag security --language ruby

# Run all new tests
uv run python test-suite/run_manifest.py \
  --case cpp-macro-traps \
  --case cpp-modern-io-clean \
  --case ruby-taint-buggy \
  --case ruby-taint-clean \
  --case ruby-serialization-buggy \
  --case ruby-serialization-clean
```

**Expected output** (all passing):
```
✅ cpp-macro-traps: 1+ warnings, substring found
✅ cpp-modern-io-clean: 0 warnings
✅ ruby-taint-buggy: 2+ critical, substrings found
✅ ruby-taint-clean: 0 warnings
✅ ruby-serialization-buggy: 1+ critical, substrings found
✅ ruby-serialization-clean: 0 warnings

All 6 tests passed!
```

Quality Assurance
=================

**Assertion coverage**:
- **Exit codes**: Verified for both success and failure paths
- **Finding counts**: Min/max assertions prevent regressions
- **Output content**: Substring assertions validate messages
- **Severity levels**: Critical vs warning distinctions

**Regression protection**:
- If macro detection breaks → cpp-macro-traps fails
- If command injection detection breaks → ruby-taint-buggy fails
- If false positives introduced → clean fixtures fail

**CI integration**:
These tests run automatically in CI, catching:
- Detection rule regressions
- False positive introductions
- Output format changes
- Module execution errors

Related Work
============

Supports commits:
- 4f4698e: test(cpp): Add macro pitfalls and modern I/O baseline fixtures
- 64eb7db: test(ruby): Add command injection security fixtures
- 3594beb: test(ruby): Add unsafe YAML deserialization fixtures

**Test coverage summary**:
```
C++ coverage:
- Macro pitfalls ✅
- Modern I/O baseline ✅

Ruby coverage:
- Command injection (system/backticks) ✅
- YAML deserialization ✅
```

**Total new fixtures**: 6 test cases, 8 source files, ~160 lines of manifest JSON
